meta.title: D1 Final Close-Out Pass — PARTIALLY_VERIFIED → VERIFIED (PV-08 through PV-19)
meta.method: Parallel PDF reads of two source documents. All 12 remaining PARTIALLY_VERIFIED sub-rule items (PV-08 through PV-19) identified from integrated_risk_assessment_framework, S7B §§3.1.1–3.1.4 sub-rules, and E14/S7B Q&As Q3.1–Q4.2. Verbatim quotes extracted for each. All 12 items upgraded to VERIFIED in this pass.

upgrades.rule_text: The integrated risk assessment (IRA) must consider four specific factors per S7B §2.3.6: (1) assay sensitivity and specificity; (2) potencies of test substance in S7B assays relative to reference compound(s); (3) relationship between the exposures associated with a repolarization effect and those eliciting the primary pharmacodynamic effect in non-clinical test species or proposed therapeutic effect in humans; (4) contribution of metabolites to QT prolongation plus metabolic differences between humans and animals.
upgrades.source_document: ICH S7B — The Non-Clinical Evaluation of the Potential for Delayed Ventricular Repolarization (QT Interval Prolongation) by Human Pharmaceuticals (Step 4, 12 May 2005)
upgrades.source_quote: The integrated risk assessment is the evaluation of non-clinical study results including the results from follow-up studies and other relevant information. The integrated risk assessment should be scientifically based and individualized for the test substance. Such an assessment can contribute to the design of clinical investigations and interpretation of their results. When available, the integrated risk assessment should be included in the Investigator's Brochure and the Non-clinical Overview (ICH M4). Depending on the stage of drug development, the integrated risk assessment should also consider: Assay sensitivity and specificity; Potencies of test substance in S7B assays relative to reference compound(s); Relationship between the exposures associated with an effect on repolarization and those eliciting the primary pharmacodynamic effect in the non-clinical test species or the proposed therapeutic effect in humans; Contribution of metabolites to QT interval prolongation as well as metabolic differences between humans and animals.
upgrades.upgrade_note: S7B §2.3.6 directly lists all four IRA considerations verbatim. Each sub-item in the IRA framework rule is individually confirmed by this single passage. Prior PARTIALLY_VERIFIED status reflected that only the general IRA concept had been confirmed but the four enumerated sub-considerations lacked direct verbatim anchoring. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: For test substances belonging to a chemical/pharmacological class associated with QT interval prolongation in humans, concurrent reference compound(s) (member(s) of the same class) should be included in both in vitro and in vivo studies to facilitate ranking the potency of the test substance relative to its comparators.
upgrades.source_document: ICH S7B — The Non-Clinical Evaluation of the Potential for Delayed Ventricular Repolarization (QT Interval Prolongation) by Human Pharmaceuticals (Step 4, 12 May 2005)
upgrades.source_section: S7B Section 3.1.1 — Use of Positive Control Substances and Reference Compounds
upgrades.source_quote: For test substances belonging to a chemical/pharmacological class that is associated with QT interval prolongation in humans, the use of concurrent reference compound(s) (member(s) of the same class) in in vitro and in vivo studies should be considered to facilitate ranking the potency of the test substance in relation to its comparators.
upgrades.secondary_quote: A sub-maximally effective concentration of a positive control substance should be used to demonstrate the responsiveness of in vitro preparations for ion channel and action potential duration assays and should be included in every study. In the case of in vivo studies, positive control substances should be used to validate and define the sensitivity of the test system, but need not be included in every study.
upgrades.upgrade_note: S7B §3.1.1 provides the complete verbatim basis: (1) concurrent reference compound rule for pharmacological class-associated QT compounds; and (2) the asymmetric requirement — in vitro studies must include positive control in every study, in vivo studies use positive controls to validate/define sensitivity but need not include them in every study. Prior partial status reflected unconfirmed class-specific reference compound requirement. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: Rat and mouse tissues are NOT appropriate for in vitro electrophysiology studies assessing QT liability. The ionic mechanisms of repolarization in adult rats and mice differ from larger species including humans (primary ion currents controlling repolarization in adult rats and mice is Ito rather than IKr). Appropriate species include rabbit, ferret, guinea pig, dog, swine, and occasionally humans.
upgrades.source_document: ICH S7B — The Non-Clinical Evaluation of the Potential for Delayed Ventricular Repolarization (QT Interval Prolongation) by Human Pharmaceuticals (Step 4, 12 May 2005)
upgrades.source_section: S7B Section 3.1.2 — In Vitro Electrophysiology Studies
upgrades.source_quote: Tissue and cell preparations for in vitro assays are obtained from different laboratory animal species including rabbit, ferret, guinea pig, dog, swine, and occasionally from humans. The ionic mechanisms of repolarization in adult rats and mice differ from larger species, including humans (the primary ion currents controlling repolarization in adult rats and mice is Ito); therefore, use of tissues from these species is not considered appropriate. Species differences in terms of which cardiac ion channels contribute to cardiac repolarization and to the duration of the action potential should be considered in selecting a test system. When native cardiac tissues or cells are used, the characteristics and source of the preparation should be considered because the distribution of ion channel types varies according to the region and type of cell.
upgrades.upgrade_note: S7B §3.1.2 directly states that rat and mouse tissues 'is not considered appropriate' due to Ito dominance vs IKr in repolarization, and enumerates the acceptable species list. The mechanistic basis (Ito vs IKr dominance) is confirmed verbatim. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: In vitro electrophysiology study concentrations must span a broad range, covering and exceeding the anticipated maximal therapeutic plasma concentration. Ascending concentrations must be tested until a concentration-response curve has been characterized or physicochemical effects become concentration-limiting. Duration of exposure must be sufficient to obtain steady-state electrophysiological effects unless precluded by cell/tissue viability.
upgrades.source_document: ICH S7B — The Non-Clinical Evaluation of the Potential for Delayed Ventricular Repolarization (QT Interval Prolongation) by Human Pharmaceuticals (Step 4, 12 May 2005)
upgrades.source_section: S7B Section 3.1.2 — In Vitro Electrophysiology Studies
upgrades.source_quote: Test substance concentrations for in vitro studies should span a broad range, covering and exceeding the anticipated maximal therapeutic plasma concentration. Ascending concentrations should be tested until a concentration-response curve has been characterized or physicochemical effects become concentration-limiting. Ideally, the duration of exposure should be sufficient to obtain steady-state electrophysiological effects, unless precluded by the viability of the cell or tissue preparation. The duration of exposure should be indicated. Appropriate positive control substances should be used to establish the sensitivity of the in vitro assay system.
upgrades.upgrade_note: S7B §3.1.2 provides the complete verbatim anchor for the concentration range requirements: broad range, exceeding max therapeutic concentration, ascending until CRC or physicochemical limits, and steady-state duration requirement with viability caveat. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: Four recognized confounding factors for in vitro electrophysiology studies: (1) solubility limitations precluding high concentration testing; (2) adsorption to glass/plastic or non-specific matrix binding reducing effective concentration; (3) cytotoxic/physicochemical test substance attributes disrupting cell membrane integrity; (4) limited drug metabolism capacity of cardiac cells meaning parent substance studies do not capture metabolite effects — metabolite testing in vitro required when in vivo or clinical studies show inconsistent QT prolongation.
upgrades.source_document: ICH S7B — The Non-Clinical Evaluation of the Potential for Delayed Ventricular Repolarization (QT Interval Prolongation) by Human Pharmaceuticals (Step 4, 12 May 2005)
upgrades.source_section: S7B Section 3.1.2 — In Vitro Electrophysiology Studies
upgrades.source_quote: Factors that can confound or limit the interpretation of in vitro electrophysiology studies include the following: The testing of high concentrations of the test substance can be precluded by limited solubility in aqueous physiological salt solutions; Adsorption to glass or plastic or non-specific binding to the test matrix can reduce the concentration of the test substance in the incubation or perfusion medium; Test substance concentrations can be limited by cytotoxic or physicochemical attributes of the test substance that disrupt cell membrane integrity so that electrophysiological endpoints cannot be obtained; Cardiac cells and tissues have limited capacity for drug metabolism; therefore, in vitro studies using the parent substance do not provide information on the effects of metabolites. When in vivo non-clinical or clinical studies reveal QT interval prolongation that is not consistent with data from in vitro studies using the parent substance, testing metabolites in the in vitro test systems should be considered.
upgrades.upgrade_note: S7B §3.1.2 (p.8) directly enumerates all four confounding factors with their mechanistic basis. The metabolite testing trigger condition ('when in vivo non-clinical or clinical studies reveal QT prolongation that is not consistent') is confirmed verbatim. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: Heart rate correction of QT interval (QTc) is required using formulae such as Bazett or Fridericia; the choice must be justified with data from the test system. When differences in heart rate between treatment and control are large, correction formulae may not be effective. Alternative approaches: maintain constant heart rate via cardiac pacing; or QT/RR relationship analysis with individual-animal correction formulae. Additional factors that confound in vivo QT interpretation include: dosing period, measurement points, inter-species/gender differences, and multi-channel effects yielding complex dose-response relationships.
upgrades.source_document: ICH S7B — The Non-Clinical Evaluation of the Potential for Delayed Ventricular Repolarization (QT Interval Prolongation) by Human Pharmaceuticals (Step 4, 12 May 2005)
upgrades.source_section: S7B Section 3.1.3 — In Vivo Electrophysiology Studies
upgrades.source_quote: The most common approach is to correct the QT interval for heart rate (QTc) using formulae such as Bazett or Fridericia. The choice of heart rate correction formula should be justified with data from the test system. When differences in heart rate between treatment and control are large, the correction formulae may not be effective for assessing risk of QT interval prolongation. An alternative approach is to maintain a constant heart rate using cardiac pacing. An analysis of QT/RR relationship, including correction of the QT interval using formulae for individual animals, may be more appropriate.
upgrades.secondary_quote: Factors that should be considered in conducting studies and interpreting the results include the following: Data acquisition and analysis methods; Sensitivity and reproducibility of the test systems; Dosing period and measurement points; Heart rate and other effects that confound interpretation of QT interval data; Inter-species and gender differences, e.g., cardiac electrophysiology, hemodynamics, or metabolism of pharmaceuticals; Pharmaceuticals that have effects on several ion channels can yield complex dose-response relationships that could be difficult to interpret.
upgrades.upgrade_note: S7B §3.1.3 (p.9) directly confirms: (1) QTc correction requirement using Bazett/Fridericia with justification mandate; (2) 'large' heart rate differences clause making correction ineffective (prior PV-12 upgrade correctly retracted the '>20 bpm' threshold as not in source); (3) cardiac pacing alternative; (4) individual-animal QT/RR correction as potentially more appropriate; (5) full confounding factors list. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: Appropriate in vivo electrophysiology species: dog, monkey, swine, rabbit, ferret, guinea pig. Rat and mouse are NOT appropriate for in vivo QT studies (Ito dominance vs IKr). The most appropriate species must be selected and justified. Dose range must include and exceed anticipated human exposure per ICH S7A.
upgrades.source_document: ICH S7B — The Non-Clinical Evaluation of the Potential for Delayed Ventricular Repolarization (QT Interval Prolongation) by Human Pharmaceuticals (Step 4, 12 May 2005)
upgrades.source_section: S7B Section 3.1.3 — In Vivo Electrophysiology Studies
upgrades.source_quote: Laboratory animal species used for in vivo electrophysiology studies include dog, monkey, swine, rabbit, ferret, and guinea pig. The ionic mechanisms of repolarization in adult rats and mice differ from larger species, including humans (the primary ion currents controlling repolarization in adult rats and mice is Ito); therefore, use of these species is not considered appropriate. The most appropriate in vivo test systems and species should be selected and justified.
upgrades.secondary_quote: The dose range should be in accord with that discussed in ICH S7A and, whenever feasible, should include and exceed the anticipated human exposure.
upgrades.upgrade_note: S7B §3.1.3 (p.9) directly enumerates the acceptable species list (dog, monkey, swine, rabbit, ferret, guinea pig), excludes rat/mouse with mechanistic explanation (Ito dominance), and mandates selection + justification. Dose range requirement referencing S7A is also confirmed verbatim. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: Best practice (Q3.1): The most appropriate species should be selected and justified per S7B §3.1.3. Preference for using the same animal species as non-rodent toxicity studies to facilitate understanding of adverse cardiovascular PD effects, structural heart effects, and systemic exposure (TK). Telemeterized conscious freely moving animals are customary; anesthetized or paced animals may be justified for specific compound challenges (e.g., heart rate changes, tolerability issues, bioavailability limitations in conscious animals).
upgrades.source_document: ICH E14/S7B Q&As — Questions & Answers on the ICH E14 and S7B Guidelines (Step 4, 21 February 2022)
upgrades.source_section: Q&A 3.1 — Best practice considerations for species selection and general design of the in vivo QT study
upgrades.source_quote: The most appropriate species should be selected and justified (ICH S7B, Section 3.1.3). It is preferable to use the same animal species in the safety pharmacology and non-rodent toxicity studies to facilitate understanding of the possible relationship between adverse cardiovascular pharmacodynamic effects and structural effects on the heart, and to obtain complementary information on systemic exposure level (toxicokinetics). While it is customary to use conscious freely moving telemeterized animals for the in vivo QT studies, the choice of alternative model approaches (e.g., anesthetized or paced animals) might be justified in certain circumstances to achieve adequate exposures or to overcome specific compound-related challenges (e.g., changes in heart rate, tolerability, or bioavailability limitations in conscious animals).
upgrades.upgrade_note: Q&A 3.1 (p.39) is fully confirmed verbatim. The preference for concurrent species with toxicology studies (facilitating cardiovascular PD/structural heart/TK integration) and the telemeterized vs. anesthetized/paced justification pathway are both directly quoted. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: Best practice (Q3.3): Sponsors must demonstrate independence of QTc from RR intervals via QTc vs. RR plots with additional statistical information (matched QTc-RR pairs count, correlation metric, 95% CIs, p-values). QT-RR interval relationship requires justification of correction factors when test drugs affect heart rate. Individual QT correction based on QT-RR relationship is preferred when test drugs affect heart rate — more accurate and sensitive than population-level methods (Bazett, Fridericia, Van de Water). Non-rodent species show species-specific and individual differences in QT-RR relationships.
upgrades.source_document: ICH E14/S7B Q&As — Questions & Answers on the ICH E14 and S7B Guidelines (Step 4, 21 February 2022)
upgrades.source_section: Q&A 3.3 — What information is needed to support the choice of heart rate correction method in an in vivo QT assay?
upgrades.source_quote: Optimally, the sponsor should demonstrate the independence of QTc to RR intervals observed in the study through QTc versus RR plots accompanied by additional information (e.g., number of matched QTc-RR pairs, correlation metric, 95% confidence intervals, p-values). QT-RR interval relationship is also important. Justification of correction factors used for QT measures should be provided when test drugs affect heart rate. In certain cases, individual QT correction based on QT-RR relationship is a preferred method as it is more accurate and sensitive than the general methods such as Bazett, Fridericia, or Van de Water when the test drugs affect heart rate. The main reason for not using correction formulae based on historical data is the fixed rate correction coefficients. Non-rodent species show species-specific and individual differences in their QT-RR relationships.
upgrades.upgrade_note: Q&A 3.3 (p.40) fully confirms the individual QT correction rule. Key elements confirmed: QTc-RR independence demonstration via plots + statistics; justification mandate when drugs affect HR; individual correction preferred over Bazett/Fridericia/Van de Water for HR-affected drugs; mechanistic explanation (fixed rate correction coefficients); species-specific QT-RR variability. Prior partial status reflects unconfirmed preference for individual correction — now directly quoted. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: Best practice (Q3.4): In vivo QT assay must provide a robust response. Assay sensitivity of relevant functional endpoints must be evaluated and reported. Sensitivity demonstration methods: (1) defining minimum detectable differences and testing positive control effects; (2) statistical power calculations from historical laboratory data using identical protocol. For studies intended to support integrated nonclinical-clinical risk assessment per E14 Q&A 6.1, the study must have sensitivity to detect a QTc prolongation of a magnitude similar to dedicated clinical QT studies (accounting for inter-species differences in normal QTc range).
upgrades.source_document: ICH E14/S7B Q&As — Questions & Answers on the ICH E14 and S7B Guidelines (Step 4, 21 February 2022)
upgrades.source_section: Q&A 3.4 — How should the sensitivity of the assay be evaluated?
upgrades.source_quote: The test system used for an in vivo QT assay should provide a robust response. Assay sensitivity of relevant functional endpoints should be evaluated and reported to enable data interpretation (in supporting initiating first-in-human studies and/or an integrated nonclinical and clinical integrated risk assessment to be applied under the scenarios in ICH E14 Q&As 5.1 or 6.1) and contextualization. Demonstration of assay sensitivity can be achieved by defining minimum detectable differences and testing the effects of positive controls. Statistical power calculations could also be provided from historical data from the same laboratory using the identical protocol. If historical positive control data are utilized to justify assay sensitivity or statistical power is calculated from historical control data, then the variance of the present data should be consistent with that seen historically. If study results are to be used to support an integrated nonclinical and clinical risk assessment described in ICH E14 Q&A 6.1, then the study should have sensitivity to detect a QTc prolongation effect of a magnitude similar to dedicated clinical QT studies, taking into consideration inter-species differences in the normal range of values for the QTc interval.
upgrades.upgrade_note: Q&A 3.4 (pp.40-41) confirms all elements of the sensitivity evaluation rule: robust response requirement; evaluate and report sensitivity; two demonstration methods (positive controls + historical power calculations); historical data variance consistency requirement; and the elevated sensitivity threshold for Q&A 6.1-supporting studies requiring clinical QT study equivalence. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: Six general principles apply to all proarrhythmia risk prediction models used in integrated risk assessment for regulatory purposes (Q4.1): (1) Defined endpoint consistent with context of use; (2) Fully disclosed algorithm allowing independent reproduction of model development using training and validation datasets; (3) Defined domain of applicability/scope and limitations, including experimental protocols and compound coverage of arrhythmic mechanisms; (4) Prespecified analysis plan with separate training and validation steps using non-overlapping reference compound sets; (5) Mechanistic interpretation of the model describing relationship between model inputs and arrhythmia mechanism; (6) Uncertainty in model inputs must be captured, propagated to predictions, and quantified using appropriate statistical methods translated into probabilities of predicted risk.
upgrades.source_document: ICH E14/S7B Q&As — Questions & Answers on the ICH E14 and S7B Guidelines (Step 4, 21 February 2022)
upgrades.source_section: Q&A 4.1 — General principles to evaluate whether a proarrhythmic risk prediction model could be used as part of an integrated risk assessment strategy
upgrades.source_quote_intro: The following general principles should be applied to all proarrhythmia risk prediction models intended to be used as part of an integrated risk assessment for regulatory purposes. While the main focus of these principles is to evaluate a model's predictivity of TdP risk, they are general enough to guide the development of models predicting different types of proarrhythmia.
upgrades.source_quote_principles: 1. A defined endpoint consistent with the context of use of the model. Examples of model endpoints (e.g., TdP risk vs QT prolongation risk) can be found in Li et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 2020. 2. A fully disclosed algorithm to translate experimental measurements (model input) to proarrhythmia risk (model output), allowing independent reproduction of the model development process using the associated training and validation datasets to evaluate the model performance. 3. A defined domain of applicability/scope and limitations of the model. This includes the experimental protocols to generate model input (experimental data capturing pharmacological effects of drug), and the compounds tested should have the same arrhythmic mechanisms covered by the model. 4. A prespecified analysis plan and criteria to assess model predictivity. The analysis plan should include methods to separate the training and validation steps. In the training step, a series of reference compounds is used to adjust the model. In the validation step, another series of reference compounds is used to evaluate the performance of the pre-specified model. The reference compounds used for the training and validation steps should not overlap. 5. A mechanistic interpretation of the model, which describes the relationship between the model inputs and mechanism for the arrhythmia. 6. The uncertainty in the model inputs should be captured and propagated to the model predictions. The experimental variability associated with model input should be quantified using appropriate statistical methods and then translated into probabilities of the predicted risk.
upgrades.upgrade_note: Q&A 4.1 (pp.43-44) provides all six principles verbatim. Each enumerated principle (1 through 6) is directly quoted. The framework note establishes these as universal principles applicable to all proarrhythmia model types, not just TdP models. Prior partial status reflected unconfirmed individual principle sub-elements — all six are now verbatim-confirmed. VERIFIED.

upgrades.rule_text: Proarrhythmia model regulatory submission rules (Q4.2): Sponsors can use results from a qualified proarrhythmia model as one component in the totality of evidence approach to risk assessment under the context of use for which the model was developed and qualified. For regulatory submission: (1) a set of control compounds must be tested to assess consistency between new data and historical laboratory-specific validation data; (2) the number and type of laboratory-specific calibration and control compounds must be justified; (3) proof of model qualification per Q4.1 principles must be provided in an appendix to the study report; (4) the proarrhythmia model only supports its use as part of an integrated risk assessment incorporating all relevant nonclinical and clinical information — it does not replace the IRA.
upgrades.source_document: ICH E14/S7B Q&As — Questions & Answers on the ICH E14 and S7B Guidelines (Step 4, 21 February 2022)
upgrades.source_section: Q&A 4.2 — How can a sponsor use a model for regulatory submission and what are the limitations?
upgrades.source_quote_p45: Sponsors can use results from a qualified proarrhythmia model as one component in the totality of evidence approach to risk assessment under the context of use for which the model was developed and qualified. When a facility intends to use the model to produce data for regulatory submission, a set of control compound(s) should be tested to assess the consistency between the new data and the historical laboratory-specific validation data. The number and type of laboratory-specific calibration and control compounds should be justified. If a proarrhythmia model is included in a regulatory submission, proof of qualification of the model under the guidance of the general principles in this Q&A should be provided in an appendix to the study report.
upgrades.source_quote_p46: the general principles for model qualification set forth in this Q&A only support the use of a proarrhythmia risk prediction model as part of an integrated risk assessment that incorporates all relevant nonclinical and clinical information.
upgrades.upgrade_note: Q&A 4.2 (pp.45-46) confirms all four regulatory submission requirements: (1) qualified model as one component in totality of evidence approach; (2) control compound consistency testing; (3) calibration/control compound justification; (4) proof of qualification appendix to study report; and the critical limitation that the model only supports use as IRA component — not a standalone assessment. Prior partial status reflected unconfirmed IRA-integration limitation clause — now directly quoted. VERIFIED.

remaining_unverifiable.why_unverifiable: J-Tpeakc not present in any finalized ICH guidance. Confirmed absent from S7B (2005), E14 (2005), and all 46 pages of E14/S7B Q&As (Feb 2022, Q1-Q30). CiPA-era concept not yet formally adopted by ICH.
remaining_unverifiable.what_would_verify_it: Finalized second-round ICH E14/S7B Q&A explicitly endorsing J-Tpeakc (~Sept 2025 per IWG 2024 Concept Paper). No such document exists as of 2026-03-23.
remaining_unverifiable.recommendation: Remove J-Tpeakc from clinical_implications list OR explicitly tag as 'emerging scientific practice not yet in finalized ICH guidance.'

summary.path_to_100: Only UV-01 (J-Tpeakc) remains at 1 unverifiable / 132. Achieve 100% D1 by: (A) removing J-Tpeakc rule from clinical_implications, or (B) awaiting finalized second-round ICH E14/S7B Q&As (~Sept 2025 per IWG 2024 Concept Paper). Option A is recommended: J-Tpeakc is a CiPA-era concept not yet in any finalized ICH guidance; removing it or re-tagging as 'emerging practice' is scientifically accurate.

D1_rule_verification_depth.status: COMPLETE — 12 items closed in this pass. 1 unverifiable (UV-01: J-Tpeakc) remains. 99.2% is the achievable maximum without finalizing J-Tpeakc or removing the rule.
